title: Lemon curd pavlova
subtitle: A crisp meringue shell filled with lemon curd and whipped cream
excerpt: Lemon curd pavlova swaps the usual fresh fruit for a generous layer of sharp lemon curd beneath the whipped cream. The curd cuts through the sweetness of the meringue and balances the richness of the cream. The method is the same as a standard pavlova; the filling is the difference.
sourceNotes: Pavlova with lemon curd is a British variation that developed alongside the standard cream-and-berry pavlova. Lemon curd as a filling for meringue is documented in British baking books from the mid-twentieth century. This recipe follows the established British formula for pavlova with a lemon curd filling layer.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the meringue shell the day before and leave in the switched-off oven overnight. Fill no more than 30 minutes before serving.

baking.decoratingTechnique: lemon curd layer, whipped cream, lemon zest

glossaryTerms.definition: A well-made pavlova has a shallow hollow in the centre that forms naturally as the meringue dries. Shape the shell with a slight dip in the middle and raised edges; the hollow deepens a little during baking and provides a natural container for the filling. A flat-topped pavlova will have the filling slide off the moment you spoon it on.

content.text: The only thing that makes this different from a standard pavlova is the order of fillings: lemon curd goes in first, directly onto the meringue, then the whipped cream on top. The contrast between the bitter-sharp curd and the sweet meringue is the point. Use a good bought lemon curd or make your own; a thin, syrupy one will not hold on the
, so look for one thick enough to hold its shape on a spoon.
Make and bake the meringue shell

Heat the oven to 120 degrees C conventional (not fan). Draw a 22 cm circle on baking paper and flip it onto a baking tray. Whisk the 4 egg whites with a quarter teaspoon cream of tartar until soft peaks form. Add the 220 g caster sugar one tablespoon at a time, whisking on high speed. Once all the sugar is in and the meringue is thick and glossy, fold in the 1 tsp cornflour and 1 tsp vinegar. Pile onto the circle, building up the edges so the centre sits lower. Bake for 1 hour 15 minutes until the surface is dry and sounds hollow when tapped. Turn off the oven and leave inside until completely cold, at least 2 hours.

Transfer the meringue to a serving plate. Spoon the 200 g lemon curd into the hollow and spread to the edges. Whip the 300 ml cream to soft peaks and spoon over the curd. Scatter the lemon zest on top if using. Serve within 30 minutes.

items.problem: Lemon curd makes the meringue soggy quickly
items.solution: The curd was too thin and absorbed into the meringue. Use a thick, set curd and fill no more than 30 minutes before serving. Storing a filled pavlova for more than an hour will always soften the shell.

items.problem: Meringue collapses when transferred to a serving plate
items.solution: It was moved while still warm. A hot meringue is fragile. Leave it to cool completely in the oven before moving. Use a wide flat spatula or a palette knife to slide it gently from the baking paper.
